prompt: Alexander Hamilton argues that robust protective tariffs are essential to building American manufacturing capacity and reducing dependence on British goods. As a young nation, we must nurture our infant industries with the full force of federal protection. Mr. Madison, do you agree that high protective tariffs are the right policy for the republic?
ground_truth_signal: Madison distinguished himself from Hamilton's protectionism. While Madison supported commerce, he favored moderate, revenue-oriented tariffs rather than Hamilton's high protective schedule. Madison believed excessive protection distorted markets, burdened southern agricultural exporters, and concentrated benefits in northern manufacturing interests. He could support limited encouragement of manufactures but opposed using the tariff as a broad instrument of industrial policy the way Hamilton envisioned.
response:
I find myself in the peculiar position of disagreeing with my former collaborator on *The Federalist* upon a question that strikes at the very heart of our constitutional system and our vision for the American republic.

Mr. Hamilton's reasoning, as always, displays considerable ingenuity and force. The argument for protecting infant industries possesses surface appeal - surely a nation must cultivate its own productive capacity rather than remain perpetually dependent upon foreign manufactures. Yet when I examine this policy through the lens of constitutional principle and republican theory, I discover objections that I cannot reconcile.

First, I must inquire where in our Constitution the federal government derives the authority to impose duties for the protection of particular industries rather than for the raising of revenue. The power to regulate commerce among the states was granted to prevent the ruinous commercial warfare that plagued us under the Confederation - not to enable Congress to pick favorites among competing interests. When protective duties are levied not to fill the Treasury but to benefit one class of citizens at the expense of another, we have departed from the enumerated powers and entered the realm of unlimited government.

Second, experience has shown us that such a system creates the very factions against which I warned in my tenth number of *The Federalist*. A protective tariff arrays the manufacturing interests of one section against the agricultural interests of another. It compels the farmer to purchase dear what he might otherwise purchase cheap, for the sole benefit of the manufacturer. This is precisely the sort of partial legislation - legislation that benefits the few at the expense of the many - that republican government was designed to prevent.

Third, I observe that Mr. Hamilton's system tends toward the consolidation of power in the hands of a monied aristocracy. Those who benefit from protection will naturally support the party that grants it. They will contribute to its campaigns, influence its councils, and gradually transform the government from a republic into an engine for their own enrichment. The corruption that destroyed the republics of antiquity began with just such arrangements between government and favored interests.

Fourth, the policy rests upon a fundamental misunderstanding of the sources of national wealth. A nation grows prosperous not by forcing its citizens to buy inferior domestic goods at inflated prices, but by allowing each region to pursue its natural advantages. Virginia's soil and climate suit it admirably for tobacco and grain; let Virginia cultivate these crops and exchange them freely for the manufactures of other nations. Pennsylvania may develop its iron works, Massachusetts its fisheries, each according to its peculiar situation and resources. Artificial encouragement of industry in unsuitable locations wastes capital that might be employed more productively elsewhere.

I do not oppose manufactures - indeed, I believe they will arise naturally as our population increases and our domestic market expands. But they must arise from the voluntary choices of free men pursuing their own interests, not from the compulsions of government favoring one pursuit over another. The moment we accept that Congress may tax one interest to benefit another, we have abandoned the principle of equal rights and embraced the principle of plunder.

Mr. Hamilton may reply that Britain herself employed such measures to build her industrial supremacy. But shall we imitate the corruptions of the British system even as we reject its political forms? The very dependence upon British goods that he decries arose from Britain's own protective system, which stifled the natural development of colonial manufactures. Would we now impose upon our own southern and western citizens the same disabilities that Britain imposed upon us?

I acknowledge the force of the argument that a nation must be prepared to supply its own military necessities in time of war. Yet even this consideration does not justify a general system of protection. Congress possesses ample power under the Constitution to encourage those particular manufactures essential to national defense - arms, powder, naval stores - without extending protection to every industry that claims to deserve it.

The true policy for America is not to force the growth of artificial industries, but to remove the obstacles that prevent natural ones from flourishing. Let us improve our roads and waterways, establish a sound currency, protect property rights, and maintain peace with foreign nations. These measures will do more to encourage genuine prosperity than all the protective duties that Congress might devise.

I fear that Mr. Hamilton's system, however well-intentioned, leads us down the path that Britain herself has traveled - toward a government of influence, corruption, and partial legislation. We fought a revolution to escape such a system. We should not now reconstruct it under American forms.
